senses.definition: A former zoological group name for certain crustaceans, now only used in specialized scientific contexts.
examples: The family was once placed in Anchistopoda, but the classification has since changed.
Older textbooks sometimes refer to Anchistopoda when describing these crustaceans.

senses.definition: An extinct type of primitive horse known from fossils, used as a technical term in paleontology.
examples: The fossil turned out to be an anchithere from the Miocene epoch.
Researchers compared the anchithere teeth to those of modern horses.

senses.definition: Relating to or resembling an anchithere, a prehistoric type of horse, in scientific descriptions.
examples: The teeth show anchitherioid features.
Anchitherioid characteristics suggest a close link with early horses.

senses.definition: A dried poblano chili pepper used in Mexican cooking, usually mild with a rich, smoky flavor.
examples: The sauce gets its deep color from roasted ancho.
You can use ancho in stews to add gentle heat.

senses.definition: Designed so that sound does not bounce back, and there are almost no echoes.
examples: The microphone was tested in an anchoic chamber.
Engineers worked in an anchoic room to measure the speaker’s true sound.

senses.definition: A heavy metal object on a chain or rope that is dropped from a boat to keep it in one place.
examples: The captain dropped the anchor near the shore.
We raised the anchor at sunrise and left the bay.

senses.definition: A person or thing that provides strong support or a feeling of safety.
examples: Her family is an anchor in difficult times.
A few close friends can be a strong anchor in your life.

senses.definition: A person who presents and coordinates a news program on television or radio.
examples: The evening news anchor interviewed the mayor.
She became the main anchor for the morning show.

senses.definition: To hold a boat in one place by dropping an anchor into the water.
examples: We anchored the boat close to the beach.
Several yachts were anchored just outside the harbor.

senses.definition: To fix something firmly in position so that it does not move.
examples: The poles are anchored in concrete for safety.
Make sure you anchor the shelf to the wall properly.

senses.definition: To present and lead a television or radio program, especially a news show.
examples: She anchors a popular morning news program.
The debate was anchored by a well-known journalist.

senses.definition: Able to be held in place with an anchor, or suitable for anchoring a vessel.
examples: The bay is not anchorable during strong storms.
We looked for an anchorable spot close to the harbor entrance.

senses.definition: An area in the sea or a harbor where ships can safely drop their anchors and stay.
examples: The ship waited at the outer anchorage until a berth was free.
This bay provides good anchorage in all weather.

senses.definition: The action of anchoring or the state of being held in place by an anchor.
examples: Anchorage of the vessel took longer than expected.
The anchorage of the structure must be checked regularly.

senses.definition: Plural of anchorage: several places where ships can safely drop their anchors and stay.
examples: The chart marks all safe anchorages along the coast.
These bays are popular anchorages for small yachts.

senses.definition: Having anchors or anchor-shaped parts, used in specialized scientific or descriptive contexts.
examples: The organism has small anchorate structures on its surface.
Anchorate hairs help the plant attach to rocks.

senses.definition: Held firmly in place, especially by an anchor or a strong connection.
examples: The yacht remained anchored all night near the pier.
The fence posts are firmly anchored in concrete.

senses.definition: Strongly based on or connected to something, especially an idea, tradition, or value.
examples: Their decisions are anchored in clear ethical principles.
The project is anchored in community needs.

senses.definition: A person or thing that anchors something, used rarely and mainly in technical or historical texts.
examples: The anchorer ensured the vessel stayed in position overnight.
Each anchorer must check the lines before the storm arrives.

senses.definition: In the past, a religious woman who chose to live alone in a small room, usually attached to a church, to pray and live apart from other people.
examples: The medieval anchoress spent her life in prayer and silence.
Pilgrims came to seek advice from the local anchoress.

senses.definition: Plural of anchoress: religious women in the past who lived alone in small rooms to pray and avoid contact with the world.
examples: In medieval Europe, anchoresses were respected spiritual figures.
Stories about anchoresses appear in several church records.

senses.definition: An old form of the word anchorite, meaning a religious person who lives alone away from other people to pray.
examples: The old text speaks of an anchoret living in the desert.
An anchoret might spend years in solitude.

senses.definition: Relating to an anchoret or anchorite, or to a very solitary religious life.
examples: He chose an anchoretic lifestyle devoted to prayer.
Anchoretic traditions influenced early monastic rules.

senses.definition: Another form of anchoretic: connected with the life of an anchoret or religious hermit.
examples: The book describes the anchoretical customs of the early church.
He wrote about anchoretical communities in the Middle Ages.

senses.definition: Somewhat like an anchoret, especially in being very solitary or avoiding society.
examples: He lived an anchoretish life in a small cabin in the woods.
Her anchoretish habits kept her away from city life.

senses.definition: The practice or way of life of an anchoret or religious hermit, living alone for spiritual reasons.
examples: Anchoretism became widespread in some early Christian regions.
The text offers a defense of anchoretism as a pure form of devotion.

senses.definition: Plural of anchoret: religious people who lived alone away from others to pray and live simply.
examples: The valley once sheltered several holy anchorets.
Stories of early anchorets inspired later monks.

senses.definition: A rare or old word for a female anchorite who lives alone for religious reasons.
examples: An anchorette occupied the small cell beside the chapel.
The story tells of an anchorette who advised local villagers.

senses.definition: Plural of anchorette: female religious hermits who live alone in small rooms or cells.
examples: Few records of these anchorettes have survived.
The town once supported three anchorettes.

senses.definition: A small room or cell where an anchorite or anchoress lives, often attached to a church.
examples: Visitors passed food to her through a window in the anchorhold.
The old anchorhold is still visible on the church wall.

senses.definition: The act of dropping or using an anchor to hold a boat or other object in place.
examples: Proper anchoring is essential in crowded harbors.
We practiced anchoring in different weather conditions.

senses.definition: Holding something firmly in position like an anchor.
examples: An anchoring belief can shape all your decisions.
We need an anchoring point for the new structure.

senses.definition: A religious person, usually in the past, who chose to live alone in a small room or place to pray and avoid contact with other people.
examples: The anchorite spent decades in a tiny stone cell.
Medieval anchorites often advised visitors through a small window.

senses.definition: Plural of anchorite: religious men or women in the past who lived alone in small places to pray and separate themselves from the world.
examples: The region was famous for its anchorites in the twelfth century.
Legends grew around the holy anchorites who lived by the river.

senses.definition: An old or rare form meaning a female anchorite who lives alone for religious reasons.
examples: A local anchoritess was believed to have healing powers.
The anchoritess gave spiritual advice through the grille.

senses.definition: Relating to the life or practices of an anchorite, especially a very solitary religious life.
examples: Anchoritic traditions shaped early monastic movements.
She wrote about anchoritic spirituality in medieval England.

senses.definition: Another form of anchoritic: connected with the life or ideals of an anchorite.
examples: The anchoritical ideal stressed solitude and prayer.
Anchoritical texts guided those who chose to withdraw from society.

senses.definition: In a way that relates to or fits the life of an anchorite, especially in great solitude.
examples: He lived anchoritically on the edge of the village.
The saint is described as having lived almost anchoritically.

senses.definition: Rather like an anchorite, especially in choosing to live very alone and separate from others.
examples: There is something anchoritish about his quiet, isolated home.
Her anchoritish ways worried her outgoing family.

senses.definition: The way of life of an anchorite, living alone for religious reasons.
examples: Anchoritism played an important role in medieval spirituality.
The rise of anchoritism reflected a desire for stricter devotion.

senses.definition: Without an anchor, or not fixed in place and free to move.
examples: An anchorless boat drifted slowly out to sea.
The raft remained anchorless and unsafe near the rocks.

senses.definition: Feeling that you have no strong support, direction, or connection in life.
examples: After losing his job, he felt completely anchorless.
The sudden move left the children anchorless and confused.

senses.definition: Having a shape or function similar to an anchor.
examples: The plant has anchorlike roots that grip the rocks.
An anchorlike device holds the frame steady.

senses.definition: A man who presents and leads a television or radio news program.
examples: He became the main anchorman of the evening news.
The anchorman introduced the top stories of the day.

senses.definition: Plural of anchorman: men who present and lead television or radio news programs.
examples: Two well-known anchormen will cover the election tonight.
Anchormen and anchorwomen from around the world met at the conference.

senses.definition: People, of any gender, who present and lead television or radio news programs.
examples: Experienced anchorpeople covered the breaking news.
The network hired several new anchorpeople this year.

senses.definition: A person, of any gender, who presents and leads a television or radio news program.
examples: She works as the anchorperson for the morning news.
The anchorperson introduced a special report on climate change.

senses.definition: Plural of anchorperson: people who present and lead television or radio news programs.
examples: Many anchorpersons attended the media workshop.
The channel replaced two of its long-time anchorpersons.

senses.definition: Plural of anchor: heavy metal objects used to keep boats in one place, or people or things that give strong support.
examples: Two anchors keep the ship steady in strong currents.
In hard times, close friends can be important anchors in your life.

senses.definition: Third person singular of anchor: holds something firmly in place or presents a broadcast.
examples: She anchors the lunchtime news every weekday.
This beam anchors the whole structure to the ground.

senses.definition: In the manner of, or with regard to, anchoring.
examples: Anchorwise, the bay offers good shelter.
Anchorwise speaking, this seabed is too rocky.

senses.definition: A woman who presents and leads a television or radio news program.
examples: The anchorwoman opened the broadcast with a breaking story.
She became the first anchorwoman on the national channel.

senses.definition: Plural of anchorwoman: women who present and lead television or radio news programs.
examples: Two well-known anchorwomen hosted the special broadcast.
Anchorwomen from several networks met at the media festival.

senses.definition: Full of anchors or having many anchors, or strongly fixed like an anchor, used rarely and mainly descriptively.
examples: The harbor floor is quite anchory and cluttered.
He gave an anchory, stabilizing presence to the team.

senses.definition: Plural of ancho: dried poblano chili peppers used in cooking.
examples: The stew is seasoned with chipotles and anchos.
Soak the anchos in hot water before blending them into the sauce.

senses.definition: A small fish similar to an anchovy, found especially off the Pacific coast of South America and often used for fishmeal.
examples: The Peruvian anchoveta supports a huge fishing industry.
Anchoveta stocks are closely monitored by scientists.

senses.definition: Plural of anchoveta: small anchovy-like fish, often caught in large numbers.
examples: The fleet landed thousands of tons of anchovetas.
Overfishing of anchovetas can damage the marine ecosystem.

senses.definition: A variant spelling of anchoveta, a small anchovy-like fish used mainly for fishmeal.
examples: The anchovetta population has declined in recent years.
Anchovetta fishing is tightly regulated.

senses.definition: Plural of anchovetta: small anchovy-like fish, often processed into fishmeal.
examples: Factories process anchovettas into fishmeal and oil.
Large shoals of anchovettas gather near the coast.

senses.definition: Small, salty fish that are often preserved in oil or salt and used to add strong flavor to food.
examples: She ordered a pizza with extra anchovies.
Anchovies are often used in sauces and dressings.
